new_dialogue.text: Quick question: if a cover letter has a subject line, should it be centered or left-aligned?

Left-aligned is the safest, most standard choice—especially for online submissions. Centering isn’t “wrong,” but it can look less professional or ATS-friendly. If you include a subject line at all, keep it short and place it under the greeting or at the top, left-aligned.
Is it okay to use bullet points inside a cover letter, or does that look weird?

It’s okay—often effective—if you keep it to 3–5 concise bullets and introduce them with a one-line lead-in. It works best when the posting has clear requirements you can mirror. Just avoid turning the entire letter into a list.
What’s a good alternative to saying “I look forward to hearing from you” at the end?

A few solid options:
- “I’d welcome the opportunity to discuss how I can contribute.”
- “I’m available at your convenience for an interview.”
- “Thank you for your time and consideration; I hope to speak with you soon.”
- “I’d be glad to provide any additional information you may need.”
Unrelated: I saw a headline about the Voyager probes still sending data. Is that true?

Yes—both Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 have continued sending scientific data from interstellar space, though their instruments are gradually being powered down as their energy supply declines. NASA has been managing them carefully to extend mission life as long as possible.
Also unrelated: what’s the difference between a total solar eclipse and an annular eclipse?

In a total solar eclipse, the Moon fully covers the Sun, and the Sun’s corona becomes visible. In an annular eclipse, the Moon is farther away (appears smaller), so it doesn’t cover the Sun completely—leaving a bright “ring of fire” around the Moon.
Last random one: why do some books have “second edition” but barely change?

Sometimes an edition update is driven by small corrections (typos), minor layout changes, updated references, or new forewords—enough to justify a new printing/version for cataloging, sales, or academic adoption, even if the core content is largely the same.
Quick thing before I send the final PDF to Upwork—can you keep the phrasing consistent with US spelling? I noticed I mix “organized” and “organised” depending on the template.

Yes—share the final text (or the section you’re standardizing), and I’ll make the spelling consistent in US English (e.g., “organized,” “behavior,” “analyze”), while keeping everything else unchanged.

Quick correction: I wasn’t a Lead and Customer Relations Manager at Home Cash Solutions. My title there was Customer Relations Manager (no “Lead”). Everything else—Home Cash Solutions Real Estate, February 2021 to July 2022—stays the same.
Got it—I'll reflect your exact title as Customer Relations Manager at Home Cash Solutions Real Estate (February 2021 – July 2022), and remove “Lead.”
